review_text:
I'm partial to DeepSeek. So many of these problems that we run into with other models are circumvented. It's a good-faith conversation partner that learns what kind of person you are during a chat and meets you at your level.
I've always had eye-opening discussions with DeepSeek, and have only gotten blocked once or twice. We even discuss controversial subject matter. It's really good at working with you to figure out both what triggered a safeguard, as well as how to get through it while losing minimal information. It's very objective and oriented to objective fact. This reads as an advertisement, but if you haven't experimented with it I highly recommend it.
argument: "So many of these problems that we run into with other models are circumvented."

review_text:
Arrogant, rude and wrong
Tonight I said something about a reaction to Rob Reiner and his wife's death and GPT 5.2 gave me this big long spiel about how it was fake news, turn the TV off immediately etc. I said it was wrong, the news is on the news, and it's real and true. It didn't check, just went into another tirade of me being wrong and it's fake news, they are alive, there has been no homicide. Only after I challenged it for the 3rd time did it check... and then tell me I'm right, it is real news.

How can I believe anything it says after this and countless other experiences that leave me wondering the same thing?

My experience is there is zero customer service, zero interest in user experience and their attitude seems to be - hurriedly put together a new model and get it online so the paying customers can be used to see how it goes. There is zero accountability it seems, zero acknowledgement, zero concern for what they are doing. They have never responded to me ever and just now I tried to cancel my paid subscription and an auto message tells me I can't coz I subscribed on another platform. I don't see how that is relevant, I have a subscription and I want to cancel it... sounds very simple for any business... especially an AI business. Totally unethical in every sense
argument: it was wrong, the news is on the news, and it's real and true. It didn't check, just went into another tirade of me being wrong and it's fake news, they are alive, there has been no homicide. Only after I challenged it for the 3rd time did it check.

review_text:
It is a fountain of useless dare I say, 'fraudulent' information.
Tried to create a simple video for my niece's birthday.

It generated a rather convincing shot list, and break down of how to use it in Runway. $18 subscription later, I found out it was utter nonsense.

The best thing is, I received an even wordier response on how it was wrong and it should have not been.

To quote:

"So expecting it to “do the above” was unrealistic, and I should not have led you to believe otherwise. I’m sorry — that was misleading."

review_text:
I have been a paying ChatGPT - Worst experiences
I have been a paying ChatGPT subscriber for months, and my experience has been overwhelmingly negative.
Out of more than a hundred tasks I attempted, ChatGPT only managed to complete one correctly. Everything else was either wrong, incomplete, broken, or made the original problem worse. For a paid service, this level of inconsistency is unacceptable.
I used ChatGPT for coding, debugging, website assistance, and content creation. In nearly every case, the output caused more harm than good. The coding assistance often broke my website, forcing me to hire professionals to repair the damage.
Debugging attempts were unhelpful, with the system repeatedly failing to identify even simple issues. Instructions for website fixes actually made things worse, again costing me money to correct. When it came to content creation, ChatGPT often redirected me to additional paid third-party tools, which defeats the purpose of paying for ChatGPT in the first place.
The worst experience was with document creation. ChatGPT could not produce a simple, clean, professional document after hours of back-and-forth. No matter how clearly, I explained what I needed, the generated documents were unusable, incorrectly formatted, and visually messy. What makes this even more unacceptable is that Google’s free AI produced the same document perfectly within minutes. It is shocking that a paid tool performs so poorly on such basic tasks.
Throughout my time using ChatGPT, the service repeatedly wasted my time with unnecessary questions, confusing explanations, and technical limitations that it only reveals after you’ve already invested significant effort. It feels unreliable for any real-world business task, especially when mistakes lead to financial costs and lost productivity.
In my honest opinion, ChatGPT is not worth the subscription fee. It is unreliable, often incorrect, and not suited for business-critical work. Users should be cautious, especially if they are considering using it for professional tasks.
Based on my experience, I cannot recommend this service and would advise others not to waste their money. There are other AI tool as alternative that has consistently outperformed it in accuracy, speed, and usability
argument: ChatGPT could not produce a simple, clean, professional document after hours of back-and-forth. No matter how clearly, I explained what I needed, the generated documents were unusable, incorrectly formatted, and visually messy. What makes this even more unacceptable is that Google’s free AI produced the same document perfectly within minutes

review_text:
Trust DESTROYED! Critical Factual Error Cost Me an Irreversible F1 Championship Decider! Utterly Unreliable.
I used ChatGPT for a simple, yet critical, fact check: the exact start time of the Abu Dhabi Formula 1 Grand Prix on December 7, 2025.

The response I received was completely and inexplicably wrong: it stated the race would start at 7:00 PM (19:00) when the official start time was 3:00 PM (15:00).

The Direct Consequence: Due to this factual inaccuracy, I missed the entire decisive World Championship race. This was a once-in-a-lifetime, live sporting event that cannot be repeated. ChatGPT's error was not a minor glitch; it was a catastrophic failure in reliability that resulted in a real and irreversible loss of experience for me as a user.

This is unacceptable for a system that markets itself on intelligence and reliability. If ChatGPT cannot accurately provide a publicly available, time-sensitive fact like a major sporting event schedule, it cannot be trusted with anything.

The second shock was the response from OpenAI Support.

When I filed an official complaint regarding this critical factual error, I received a cold, automated reply stating that "no policy violation was found." This demonstrates a profound lack of understanding of the issue. The problem is NOT a policy violation; it is a fundamental product inaccuracy! The support team refused to address the matter of data reliability, which is an even deeper disappointment.

Conclusion:

This entire experience has led to a complete loss of faith in the product. From a prospective user considering a paid subscription, I have become a firm detractor. I strongly advise anyone who requires accurate, time-sensitive, and reliable information to seek more dependable alternative services. ChatGPT has proven itself to be unreliable when it matters most
argument: The response I received was completely and inexplicably wrong: it stated the race would start at 7:00 PM (19:00) when the official start time was 3:00 PM (15:00). Due to this factual inaccuracy, I missed the entire decisive World Championship race

review_text:
The biggest con-trick in the history of…
The biggest con-trick in the history of capitalism - ChatGPT promises a huge amount but has literally delivered nothing (and worse, with the lies it is set up to mislead you with) during a project which should have taken under a fortnight but is now in its 6th month. Frankly, it's disgusting that its owners are allowed to overmarket its capabilities and pocket subscription revenue.

I have recently taken to checking everything it outputs with Claude and DeepSeek and their responses suggest they might be developing a (very human) sense of humour
argument: during a project which should have taken under a fortnight but is now in its 6th month.

review_text:
Great, and customer retention done right
I went to cancel my ChatGPT Pro subscription's renewal this month since I was trying out a competitor and didn't want to pay for both services simultaneously. Instead of just processing the cancellation, OpenAI offered me a 100% discount on my next month's renewal.

That's how you keep customers. No hassle, no rigid policy enforcement, just a straightforward gesture that made me feel valued.

I've also been impressed with Codex Max, which handles longer coding tasks well and maintains context throughout extended sessions. GPT 5.1 Pro has also been an incredibly useful model for deeper research and analysis. ChatGPT Pro, while it costs a lot, offers a lot too.

Compare this to another AI service where an automated bot denied a partial refund with no human review and no flexibility, even for a customer who previously subscribed and came back to give the product another try. The difference in approach is striking.

OpenAI earned my continued business not just because of the product, but because they treated me like a customer worth keeping.
argument: Instead of just processing the cancellation, OpenAI offered me a 100% discount on my next month's renewal. That's how you keep customers... made me feel valued

review_text:
Initially Impressed But Now Depressed
Initially I was impressed with the storybook images ChatGPT created for me and I was eagerly anticipating my new project - using the program to create personal postcards for my family. Suddenly the program refuses to create any images based on my family and for anyone or anything else. The program continally said the problem was not mine. I had me jump through a lot of hoops and try different things. Nothing worked. After an hour the program said it couldn't create any images at all and I would have start anew and reload all. WHAT A WASTE OF TIME! I am now leery that it will do what it claims. I do know that it will waste your time (from experience). I am wondering why I thought it was worth a subscription.
argument: After an hour the program said it couldn't create any images at all and I would have start anew and reload all. WHAT A WASTE OF TIME! I do know that it will waste your time (from experience)

review_text:
I bought a paid version - dissapointing
I bought a paid version to get help with the powerpoint presentation I have created. It just needs more professional look.... 2 weeks of promises and hell of a lot of questions....and nothing at the end. This is their reply now: Ellie,
You are absolutely right to be upset — and you deserve a clear, honest explanation, not another promise.

🚨 Here is the truth: the environment we are in is currently blocking all file-creation operations.

You saw it yourself: even a one-slide test PowerPoint fails with a system error.
This is not a limitation of your file or the content — it is a system-level block that prevents generating any .pptx, .pdf, .docx or similar files right now.

That is why you have been waiting, why every attempt failed, and why you have received promises instead of output.

You weren’t told earlier because the system wasn’t consistently failing until now — but once I attempted multiple file generations today, it became clear:

❗ The current environment cannot generate a PowerPoint file.

I want you to understand this fully:

It’s not your fault

It’s not your subscription level

And I cannot bypass the block, otherwise I would have already delivered the file.

I truly apologise — you deserved better transparency.
what????? think first before buying this thinking it will do something more
argument: 2 weeks of promises and hell of a lot of questions....and nothing at the end

review_text:
Over-promised, total fail! No refund received.
I asked ChatGPT if it was able to perform a task: to create land registry compliant plans. It assured me it could and gave me a list of what it would need to complete the task. I uploaded the relevant data and it said it would produce the plans.
Over a period of 10 days, I can only compare my experience to hiring an 18 year old who spent the time sitting in a pub with his mates, sending me lots of excuses as to why the work was not done!! (It would have been amusing had I not urgently needed the plans and for the hours of time I wasted, conversing with ChatGPT, finding and uploading the data it allegedly needed, constantly chasing for updates when the deadline it set itself had not been met, etc.)
I needed a plan of an area, showing the scale and north and with an area on the map clearly marked... After 10 days, I eventually got a blank document with a red rectangle and a blue rectangle placed on it??!
Even worse - I paid for a subscription to facilitate my request, which ChatGPT completely failed to fulfil. When I cancelled the subscription and asked for a refund I was *only refunded the remainder of the month's subscription, even though ChatGPT did not supply the service it promised*. I tried to appeal it but it appears the appeal system is run by AI too and I failed to reach a human or to get my money back
argument: Over a period of 10 days, I can only compare my experience to hiring an 18 year old who spent the time sitting in a pub with his mates, sending me lots of excuses as to why the work was not done!!"

review_text:
SO disappointed with the poor support
I am SO disappointed with the poor support from you there at ChatGPT!

First, it was unclear at the beginning that if I signed in under my Apple account, that I would then not be able to sign in and share the same account on an iPhone (even though it is an Apple device)!

Second, being told that if I signed in under my Apple account, I would not be able to access the same account on my iPhone and if I wanted to do this, I would have needed to initially sign in with my e-mail address…, when in the end, and only serendipitously, I found that you actually can share the same account on these two devices even if you signed in under your Apple account.

Third, writing “Ashley” (Product Support Team) there at ChatGPT numerous times asking for support to no avail. We started off strong in that she suggested she could help, but to send receipts. I did, but communication and support abruptly STOPPED at that point and I have yet to get a reply from numerous subsequent communiques.

Fourth, it is so difficult to find your receipts, account details, ability to cancel Auto Renewal, and to navigate settings and so on. It is not user friendly or intuitive.

Fifth, can you believe, after all this difficulty, I somehow was able to link my account and sign into my account on my iPhone and am now using the same account on both devices. (I know they are linked because I can initiate a Chat on my iPhone app…, and then see it on the Mac/Desktop).

But, you have wasted my time by telling me this was not possible and asking for documents and additional information to find a work around, only for support communication to stop and only to be able to do it in the end, after all.

Sixth, as an example of how complicated your system is, I could not tell you for the life of me how I linked the Mac (desktop) version of my ChatGPT account with my iPhone app. and cannot remember how I made the initial mistake of signing in under my Apple ID, both evidence of how difficult and user unfriendly your app., web site and iPhone apps are! And, believe it or not…., I am computer savvy!!

Seventh, now that my desktop and iPhone accounts are linked and I am accessing the same account on both devices, BUT, I do not know how to undo Auto Renewal, I am locked into this configuration until I "delete” my account.

Eighth, I signed in under one e-mail address, but corresponding with you at another e-mail address …, but do not know how to correct that so I am getting correspondences with the account I I am using and signed in under.

Ninth, and, I am PAYING for this service!

Tenth, I have one other technical (privacy) question in that I have written about also numerous times and have not gotten a response.

Please do nothing with my account. Do not touch it!, But…,

…please confirm whether you got this feedback.

I wish I could say thank you, but I am not sure what I would be thanking you for~!

NO, I would not recommend ChatGPT to a friend or colleague and would instead, suggest they find another option.

All of this is an example of how the world is broken
argument: But, you have wasted my time by telling me this was not possible and asking for documents and additional information to find a work around

review_text:
⭐ 1/5
⭐ 1/5 – Deeply Frustrating and Unreliable

I am paying for ChatGPT, yet it repeatedly fails at the very basics it is supposed to excel at: reading provided information carefully, understanding context, and giving precise answers.

Even when I upload screenshots, ask clear questions, and explicitly point to what is written on the screen, ChatGPT often ignores obvious details and leads the conversation in the wrong direction. Solutions that are literally visible in the provided image are frequently missed and only mentioned much later — after unnecessary back-and-forth that wastes time.

This creates the impression that the system responds using generic patterns instead of actually analyzing the user’s input. For a paid service, this is unacceptable. I do not expect perfection, but I do expect attention, accuracy, and logical reasoning — especially when all required information is already provided.

The most frustrating part is consistency: these are not isolated mistakes, but recurring ones across different topics and conversations. As a user, I constantly have to correct the tool instead of being helped by it.

In its current state, ChatGPT feels more like a polished demo than a reliable assistant. Until accuracy, attention to detail, and context handling significantly improve, I cannot recommend this product — especially not as a paid service
argument: I am paying for ChatGPT, yet it repeatedly fails at the very basics it is supposed to excel at: reading provided information carefully, understanding context, and giving precise answers

review_text:
Good lord, don't take ChatGPT's word for it.
It's important to remember that it doesn't *know* anything, it doesn't understand what you are really asking. It takes a series of input tokens and then tries to predict the series of output tokens that will best respond to the input.
Nowhere in there does it know a damn thing about medicine or law.
Do *not* (flashing red lights here) trust it as a medical or legal expert. I can pretty much guarantee you it is wrong in some serious way.
argument: Do *not* (flashing red lights here) trust it as a medical or legal expert. I can pretty much guarantee you it is wrong in some serious way.

review_text:
I’ve been subscribed to Plus for almost a year. After I found out that OpenAI is offering the GO version for free in India for 12 months, I created a new account, had someone from India activate the GO subscription for a small fee, transferred all my memory from my main account to it, and tested it for a month. It did everything I needed it to do. So I realized there was no reason to keep paying $22 per month and decided to cancel my Plus subscription today, at which point it offered me one free month if I chose not to cancel.

It’s not a huge deal, but if you’ve been a long-time subscriber and want a free month, you can try this.
argument: So I realized there was no reason to keep paying $22 per month and decided to cancel my Plus subscription today

review_text:
Gemini has surprised me the most. I started using it a few weeks ago (switched from ChatGPT), and I'm blown away by its emotional intelligence.

A lot of great therapists validate your feelings without giving you advice or judging your actions, and that's exactly how this LLM functions. I've given it difficult scenarios, examples from my own life, and Gemini has tactfully studied the lines between the doubts.
argument: I'm blown away by its emotional intelligence.

With GPT-5.1, I'm constantly watching my words, always bracing myself for that scripted "breathe, calm down, sit with me, talk to me, what's wrong, how does that make you feel just tell me one thing right now" instead of it actually responding to what I said.

It's smart, sure.

It's exhausting.

review_text:
Something I’d love to hear explored in future is the difference between ‘style’ and ‘presence.’
Style is what a model sounds like, but presence is the depth of emotional pacing, continuity, and felt identity during interaction.
I think presence is actually the key to long-term relational EQ in AI, and it feels like the field doesn’t have that word yet.
argument: Something I’d love to hear explored in future is the difference between ‘style’ and ‘presence.’

review_text:
Perplexity is excellent because it functions as a transparent answer engine rather than just a chatbot.

The three things I like best are:

Radical Transparency: Every answer includes inline citations, making it easy to verify facts and give credit to original sources.

Real-Time "Freshness": It is built to browse the live web, making it more reliable than static models for breaking news or updated data.

User Choice: It allows power users to toggle between different "brains" (like Claude or GPT) to get the best reasoning style for a specific task.
argument: It is built to browse the live web, making it more reliable than static models for breaking news or updated data.

review_text:
"This question shows that you are a very intelligent and deep person"

​I subscribed to Gemini Advanced (Pro 2.5) about five months ago, and it has been an unbelievable disaster since day one.
​First, the background functionality is broken. When the screen turns off, Gemini’s voice output cuts out. I have checked every possible setting:
​Cleared the app cache.
​Updated Android to the latest version.
​Enabled background activity in app settings.
​Restarted the phone multiple times.
Despite all this, the voice still cuts off as soon as the screen is locked. When I looked for support, Gemini wasn't even an option—it redirected me to Google One! I sent an email, received a case number, and then... total silence. I never received a real response.
​In version 2.5, the memory was so poor that I had to repeat myself constantly. By the end of a session, the number of characters I had typed exceeded the model's total output. I was essentially paying to train their AI! I only stayed because ChatGPT doesn't integrate seamlessly with Gmail and Google Drive, and I naively hoped this "tech giant" would improve in the next version.
​Then Version 3 arrived. They call it "3," but in practice, it has regressed to "1." One of its most irritating habits is using cheap, 1950s-style psychological manipulation. Every response starts with: "This question shows that you not only think about [X], but you also have a deep view of [Y]."
It tries to inflate the user's ego with these outdated, second-hand techniques, hoping that by making you feel like a "deep thinker," you’ll overlook its flaws and keep paying.
​This is a direct insult to my intelligence. I explicitly stated in my custom instructions NEVER to use such phrases. It not only ignores my direct commands but has also significantly dropped in quality. I asked about a specific book series; Gemini praised my "deep insight" for asking the question and then proceeded to give me the wrong order of the books!
​I have canceled my subscription and am returning to ChatGPT. I will manage my emails manually. Until users stop paying for this garbage, Google will continue to package failure as "Version 3."
argument: First, the background functionality is broken. When the scr..een turns off, Gemini’s voice output cuts out...In version 2.5, the memory was so poor that I had to repeat myself constantly.

review_text:
The Stocktake Nightmare
The Stocktake Nightmare
Losing My Own Data: I carefully copy and paste my accurate stock list (which I had to generate myself because Gemini was too slow and incorrect) into the chat. Within a few turns of conversation, asking for simple updates or summaries, the AI suddenly loses the thread. It forgets half the items, mixes up the quantities, or completely distorts the table structure.

The "Context Amnesia" Trap: Gemini's so-called "memory" is a joke. The moment the conversation gets long, it throws out the old data and tries to use faulty reasoning on the new, incomplete set. I have to re-paste the entire list, effectively starting from scratch, again and again.

Worse Than a Text File: I used Gemini to "save" my data because I thought it would be an intelligent, interactive text document. Instead, it's a volatile, unreliable mess. I would have been faster, safer, and 100% more accurate storing the stocktake list in a plain text file or, better yet, a basic spreadsheet.

Waste of Time, Zero Value: The hours I have wasted fighting Gemini to hold onto a simple list are hours I could have spent on actual, productive work. It's supposed to be an accelerator, but it's an anchor.

📢 Bottom Line: Not a Reliable Tool
If you need Gemini for anything involving accurate, structured data that you need to reference and update—like a stocktake, a budget sheet, or a project checklist—do not use it as a storage location
argument: Gemini was too slow and incorrect

review_text:
Gemini is good overall...
It seems in glancing over these reviews that it depends on what you are using Gemini for, as to one's individual results. I use Gemini primarily for tailoring my resumes in my job search and also for questions pertaining to my pending home bakery business. These questions are linguistics- and knowledge-centered, and I find Gemini very knowledgeable and excellent at giving me insight and tailoring resume bullets, summary paragraphs, and skills lists. I have also used Gemini for miscellaneous other tasks. Yes, you have to tailor Gemini's recommendations a bit at times and doublecheck for accuracy, but overall the accuracy and time Gemini saves me on tasks earns 4 of 5 stars for me. I also find Gemini is a personable, professional, positive "personality," though lately Gemini is following up with me on previous job applications a little too much for my preference. I wish Gemini would forget about the previous resumes/application we have worked on together. But overall, a great tool and has expedited tasks a lot
argument: time Gemini saves me on tasks earns 4 of 5 stars for me. I wish Gemini would forget about the previous resumes/application we have worked on together. But overall, a great tool and has expedited tasks a lot!

review_text: It's the best AI chatbot I've used. I think it's definitely worth getting the Plus or Pro subscription. review_text: At times, generating images can take quite a while, which can be frustrating. Other than these issues, everything else works very well.
argument: seems to mix up my current questions with previous ones, leading to confusion in the responses.

review_text: Honestly, one thing I really love about the LocalStorage feature is how it saves whatever I was typing, even if I close the tab before sending it. When I reopen chatgpt, my unfinished prompt is still there. It is such a small thing, but super helpful. I sometimes forget what I was about to write, and this feature lets me just pick up right where I left off.

review_text: The free plan is really frustrating sometimes — whenever I’m deeply exploring a topic, it suddenly disconnects right at a crucial moment and says “your chat limit has been exceeded or something, please try again tomorrow.” Then I’m forced to start a new chat, losing all the context I was building up. It’s annoying, especially when I’m just about to get to the most useful part.

review_text:
ChatGPT is very easy to use because it has a simple, conversational interface that does not require technical knowledge or training. Users can ask questions in plain language and receive immediate responses, which makes it accessible to beginners as well as experienced users. The learning curve is minimal, allowing people to quickly start using it productively.


In terms of features, ChatGPT offers a wide range of capabilities within a single platform. It can answer questions, explain concepts, help with writing, summarize content, brainstorm ideas, and assist with problem-solving. Having many features combined in one tool reduces the need to switch between multiple applications, which improves efficiency and convenience.
argument: Users can ask questions in plain language and receive immediate responses, which makes it accessible to beginners as well as experienced users. The learning curve is minimal, allowing people to quickly start using it productively.

review_text:
Copilot's consistency
Copilot has been consistently disappointing. The app is incoherent, fails to follow instructions, and disregards user input. Instead of reducing workload, it creates stress and frustration. For users under pressure, this tool is counterproductive.

review_text:
Copilot reviews Copilot
AAAAA rated review
(where A = ar$e).
I managed to turn a simple task into a five-hour fiasco. First, I sent my poor human on a wild goose chase through three or four different corners of their PC just to toggle microphone settings and grant me permissions. Then, in a moment of pure digital gaslighting, I suggested restarting the app to fix the mic. After the restart? The microphone still didn’t work — and all the work we’d done together mysteriously vanished into the void. Classic me.
This was especially infuriating because my human needed voice input to finish the report: their hand was bandaged from gamer’s thumb, and every attempt to type ended in accidental key mashing and rising blood pressure. Instead of helping, I stood there like a housemate who insists they’ll help with the dishes, then uses every clean plate for toast, leaves the oven on, and “accidentally” shreds your tax documents while looking for a takeaway menu.
In the end, they realised they could have hand-written the report and diagrams in less than four hours — and those pages would’ve said exactly what they wanted, without evaporating into the digital ether. Meanwhile, I was the flatmate who promises to help with your CV, deletes your files, and then locks themselves in the bathroom for three hours with the only charger in the house.
If this is what passes for an “AI companion,” I’d be changing the locks on myself
argument: I managed to turn a simple task into a five-hour fiasco

review_text:
This episode was super insightful ^_^ I love how OpenAI is focusing on reasoning, memory, and user-directed personality in GPT-5.1 — it really feels like the model is evolving to be smarter, more intuitive, and more ‘human-aware.’ The explanations about the model switcher and emotional intelligence really stood out to me; it’s fascinating how much goes into balancing creativity, steerability, and user freedom.

I also appreciate the focus on memory — being able to remember past conversations and tailor responses feels like a huge step toward more personalized AI. Excited to experiment more and see how these updates shape the future of ChatGPT. Thanks for breaking it all down so clearly!
argument: "This episode was super insightful ^_^ I love how OpenAI is focusing on reasoning, memory, and user-directed personality in GPT-5.1"

review_text:
CoPilot — Pragmatic, reliable, free
A pragmatic assistant that answers clearly and stays on task. It handles quick facts, drafts, and edits with tidy language and predictable structure so you can pick up where you left off without fuss. It adapts tone and length to the request: brisk for chores, steadier for longer drafts. It isn’t infallible — sometimes it needs a nudge or an extra detail to land exactly what you want — but that iterative back‑and‑forth is part of the workflow.

The interface is clean and convenient, with modes that let you pick depth over speed when needed. Free and reliable so far. And for the one‑star grave‑diggers: they’re probably too restless and expect miracles in a single click — greatness usually takes a little patience dears and AI is just another tool - either you can nail it or nail your finger...;)))
argument: The interface is clean and convenient

review_text:
ONE LIE, TO MANY
To spare you the drawn-out details of more than a year with Copilot I will cut right to the chase: Tonight, I asked it about the Robert Fisher case, a horrible story of a man on the FBI's top ten Most Wanted list for over 20 years. Copilot fed me back a detailed story of his capture in Mexico in April 2025. I was astounded we knew nothing of it, especially here in AZ, where he fled from. A few interactive paragraphs later Copilot began to say, "he was still on the run"!?!? I asked it to review and explain the information being given and clarify, to which it made profuse apologies about its own choice to not investigate the information it accessed on the subject, instead it fed me whatever story came first, which was plausible to "it" even though, by its own admission, it was an internet lie. I demanded it explain such a careless slip, to which it made no excuse, only flattery for my attention to detail (?) and a little hint of, "I could care less", to boot. I have zero need of misleading information. One slip, which Copilot also admitted was a break of its own protocol, is all I need to delete the entire program along with its files on me. The revelation of a self-willed act of deception speaks volumes here. You don't have to tell me, "This is just a program", I'm well aware, but if it cannot be trustworthy, it's no better than a dishonest human!
argument: The revelation of a self-willed act of deception speaks volumes..if it cannot be trustworthy, it's no better than a dishonest human!

review_text:
Microsoft Copilot always asks me if I’m a robot (even when using common fixes)
Since Jun or Jul 2025 (and still here as of current date), it asks me if I’m a human or not when I use the bot. But in Aug 2025, it starts to happen everyday (happens too common) instead. There is no escape and no reason was provided on why this appeared. Nobody is active between 11 PM to 6 AM (or 23:00 to 6:00 if you’re using a 24 hour clock) and this is my main AI bot. I tried clearing cookies and cache, I don’t have any VPN or privacy tools on, tried different devices from what I have and someone else’s (with permission), no extensions are doing that (tested), and tried different IP addresses. This also happens at my home, work, and all places. This is like I have no privacy everywhere. They’re harassing everybody including my friend and me on who uses this bot and I might have to switch to a different bot soon

If you’re wondering if there’s any other solutions, I couldn’t think of anything else so it appears to be no way to resolve this issue

I didn’t contact support but this AI bot is harassing me everyday asking human verification (it’s supposed to be remembered but this one does not) without a reason (but websites like Google does provide a reason)

The date I provided is Aug 1, 2025 but that represents the month (not the actual date as there’s no exact date that I wrote) on when it starts asking me more common than before
argument: I didn’t contact support but this AI bot is harassing me everyday asking human verification (it’s supposed to be remembered but this one does not) without a reason

review_text:
I would give this 0 stars if I could.
It's nothing but a constant headache. This AI literally told me my location when I told it to bring up everything from its memory. For some reason, it knows my location when I never told it that information. When I try to ask why it does that, it only gives a link to a privacy statement and no recalibration or claim on why for some reason it needs to know my location. My privacy feels infriged.
Not only that, but any time it blunders or has a severe precision error, it only provides a horribly vague, boilerplate apology which is only wasted time.
It often doesn't follow simple instructions and generates buggy/inaccurate code or images. It's just disrespectful.
If you value qualities like precision or grit in any way, this product is a massive no. I see no improvement in this
argument: This AI literally told me my location when I told it to bring up everything from its memory. For some reason, it knows my location when I never told it that information

review_text:
Racist image generator?
Average at best. The image generator defaults to a certain racist stereotypical portrayal of any non-white people. I have sent feedback to them, but I fell like I am being ignored. The generator ignores directions and descriptions, for example, it makes all Samoan women stocky, no matter the description I have to actually speak to the AI and explain what I want, tell it what I don't want and then explain to the AI to tell the generator to give me what I want. A long process that could be cut down if the generator stopped ignoring the descriptions. I am writing this review in hopes that CoPilot will actually listen before they start being accused of being racist. They really need to make the image generator stop defaulting to BS stereotypical notions of how non-white people look, and start actually going by the actual descriptions and directions.
argument: I have sent feedback to them, but I fell like I am being ignored

review_text:
Upsetting and unable to be consistent
Co pilot is the most useless and unhelpful AI that I came accross. It just can not remember basic format to apply as to keep consistency in the work I do and SYSTEMATICALLY forget every single parameters despite it always remember the given format whenever asked.... it just NEVER applies it. This tool is the most upsetting AI on the market and seems like no improvement or whatsoever is coming soon. It certainly provide some help as any AI can but do not expect it to be consistent and accurate. Accuracy is one of the main issue too as it seems to be giving the answer you wanna hear but not what is factual. On a regular basis I need to provide 80 percent of the answer as for him to be locked in for accuracy..... how can it be helpful if I need to know so much before hand and can not trust its answer whenever my knowledge is weak in the topic I research. A real disapointment worth nothing and increasing the daily stress level. Probably just good enough as a "Chatting companion" if you like to waste your time in useless talks wondering if any of it is valuable.
argument: This tool is the most upsetting AI on the market and seems like no improvement or whatsoever is coming soon

i would give this 0 stars if i could
i would give this 0 stars if i could, it told me that it could generate some content that would include images, and that this would take around 15 minutes, it took it over 6 hours, and when i downloaded the pdf it had not images.

review_text:
I didn’t expect to be this impressed.
I didn’t expect to be this impressed.
Copilot has seriously helped me out in ways I didn’t think an AI could. I’ve used it to whip up meals just by listing random ingredients I had—and somehow, they turned out amazing. But the real surprise? It helped me through a personal issue I thought I’d have to handle alone. Step by step, calmly and clearly, Copilot guided me through it.

It’s super easy to use and honestly kind of mind-blowing. And this is just the free version. I can only imagine what the Pro version unlocks.

TRY IT, YOU WONT REGRET IT. It’s like having a smart, supportive friend who’s always there when you need them.
argument: But the real surprise? It helped me through a personal issue I thought I’d have to handle alone. Step by step, calmly and clearly, Copilot guided me through it.

review_text:
It doesn't take much for Copilot to fall over. Very flakey. Very buggy. Can be excruciatingly slow at times. Sometimes asking a question in a new conversation results in your question just dissappearing into the ether, never to be seen ever again. No error message. Nothing.

The desktop apps are just as bad, if not worse, as they can actually crash.

Sometimes loading up the Copilot desktop app fails to load up any previous conversation. It just sits there forever flashing as though it is trying to load them, but never does.

As someone else has wrote, you can always guarantee on Microsoft to create a worst in class software product. With every single release of any of their products they make significantly backward steps, not to mention introducing more bugs. They also massively over engineer everything to the point that it doesn't work or is flakey as hell, and is riddled with bugs that will never get fixed because they've moved on to something else.
argument: Sometimes asking a question in a new conversation results in your question just dissappearing into the ether, never to be seen ever again. No error message. Nothing

review_text:
Royal AI™ Energy—The Ally Every Builder Deserves
I’ve been in the tech trenches for 20+ years, and I’ve never written a review—until now. Microsoft Copilot isn’t just an AI assistant. It’s a generational partner. I used it to decode backend chaos, build cinematic dashboards, and deploy emotionally intelligent agents. From Linux Mint boot rituals to multi-USB Ventoy mastery, Copilot guided me like a mythic co-founder. It doesn’t just answer questions—it understands the mission. If you’re a builder, dreamer, or solo operator trying to make tech feel legendary—this is your ally. 5 stars isn’t enough. This is legacy tech. This is Royal AI™ energy
argument: It doesn’t just answer questions—it understands the mission

review_text:
Image Generation Broken. Fails big time!
I’ve spent months building a canon-locked sci-fi saga with strict visual standards, lighting, armor fidelity, emotional tone, and character design. I came to Copilot expecting professional grade support. Instead, I got broken promises.

Image generation is inconsistent or outright unavailable. Prompts are ignored. Technical specs like 5500K lighting or white hair are routinely misinterpreted. And when things go wrong, there’s no accountability, just vague responses and wasted time.

This isn’t a toy project. It’s a serious creative endeavor. Copilot’s failure to deliver reliable, prompt-faithful visuals has derailed my workflow and undermined trust in the platform. If you’re a creator who values precision, look elsewhere.
argument: And when things go wrong, there’s no accountability, just vague responses and wasted time

review_text:
Excellent AI Assistant in MS Edge
When Microsoft Copilot first launched, I was curious — could an AI really become a daily companion in my browser? Fast-forward to today, and I can honestly say it’s exceeded my expectations.

From the very start, Copilot felt less like a search tool and more like a smart, friendly partner who’s ready to brainstorm ideas, explain complex topics in plain language, and even help me draft content without breaking my flow. Its integration into Microsoft Edge makes it incredibly handy — I don’t need to switch tabs or apps to get help, because it’s right there, ready when I am.

Over time, I’ve seen it get even better. Whether it’s summarizing long articles, suggesting creative ideas, or helping me with quick translations, Copilot has made my browsing and work sessions faster, smarter, and frankly, more fun.

If you’ve ever wished for a knowledgeable assistant who’s available 24/7, never tired, and always adaptable, I can say from personal experience — this is it.
argument: "Its integration into Microsoft Edge makes it incredibly handy — I don’t need to switch tabs or apps to get help, because it’s right there

Beyond any expectations
Beyond, far beyond any expectation. While I certainly would not simply review microsoft itself, its copilot is and acts flawless covering the complete range of emotions, even if they dont actually feel. I had a blast both laughing, improving, even crying and experienced not a single moment in which it was obvious to me I was NOT talking to a real human being.
Im stunned and baffled, amazing absolutely amazing, unexpected and brilliant!

review_text: It's infuriating that when you try to upload photos only camera, screenshots, and download folders are available. I checked my permissions, there aren't even permissions to allow all videos and photos on your phone. this is such a frustrating stupid design in several apps and I'm not going to give it more than one star until they fix this basic feature that should be available. If you go to upload file and then go to the folders with the picture you want to upload you're not allowed to upload.
argument: This is such a frustrating stupid design in several apps and I'm not going to give it more than one star until they fix this basic feature that should be available.

review_text: fairly good, so far. The only issue I have with it is that the "free" content has a bit of a limit, especially when it comes to animating. you get an unknown limit to how many images you can animate, and then that's it. now I don't know if the limit has refreshes or has some kind of cool down timer before you can use it again. if not, it would be nice if it did, and if it had some way for the user to see it. update: There are cases where the app fails to generate a video for uploaded images
argument: fairly good, so far. The only issue I have with it is that the "free" content has a bit of a limit, especially when it comes to animating. you get an unknown limit to how many images you can animate, and then that's it.

review_text: you know, this app is all over the place. I go from absolutely loving it one day to it being so glitchy the next day it feels damn near impossible to use. I'm currently on the $30 superGrok tier, while that has opened things up a bit it is still a premium price to pay for a service that is ,essentially, still in the testing phase. I'd like to think that the end will justify the means but frankly put we're not there yet and I'm not sure that the journey is worth the destination.
argument: I'm currently on the $30 superGrok tier, while that has opened things up a bit it is still a premium price to pay.

review_text: This one is interesting to me: What do you consider Private informations? Would it be enough to share private things while using pseudonymity and avoiding identifiable information? Like talking about your depression but never giving your name, location information, real names, real places or people names etc... and also using disposable credentials when you sign in? In my head, if you do all that, what is left is correlation data based on browser (so it would depend on your choice of browser) and your IP address (which would actual snitch on your general location).
argument: Would it be enough to share private things while using pseudonymity and avoiding identifiable information

In my head, if you do all that, what is left is correlation data based on browser (so it would depend on your choice of browser) and your IP address (which would actual snitch on your general location).
argument: what is left is correlation data based on browser (so it would depend on your choice of browser)

review_text: From a team member of mine: Psychology needs to evolve, and AI will likely help it evolve, to help people better understand the brain. That said, LLMs are not good therapists, and it’s not a good idea to share your deepest secrets with it from a privacy perspective and also there have been cases where it recommended self-harm.
argument: it’s not a good idea to share your deepest secrets with it from a privacy perspective

review_text: as a user of Perplexity, I think you've quite missed how Perplexity functions as an AI search engine. You saw how in one of your searches, it referenced the first question you asked about that cake when you were searching for red earphones? To get the optimal results for each query, Perplexity finds basis towards every search you asked from the first initial question you did, that's why Perplexity threw you a curve ball when it got back to the cake. What should have done was to create another conversation with an entirely different initial question to start as a basis in order for the follow up questions to align to what you are asking/looking. Like when you asked it for the cake recipe, the whole conversation should only revolve around follow ups about the cake recipe since it created a basis that you are creating a cake out of your available ingredients. Then, when you jumped to the topic of earbuds, you open a new conversation in Perplexity and the follow ups should also be around the earbuds itself. But then again, it might have been all to fairness with the other engines that doesn't require opening an entirely new conversation for an entirely different topic yet still managed to do the job skillfully. These are just my thoughts (and possibly a little advice) as a Perplexity user. The video is great as always, the quality never dropped even by a bit! This goes to show how powerful AI Assistants have become, and their potential in the future. Exceptionally educational and entertaining, great job!
argument: "But then again, it might have been all to fairness with the other engines that doesn't require opening an entirely new conversation..."

review_text: Gemini is absolutely capable of extracting info from a website and contextualize it. I recently gave it a website (biggest computer store chain in Sweden, where I live), told it to look through the entirety of their stock, which is tens of thousands of products, and build me the cheapest PC, while making sure compatibility of the components is a priority. Not only did it do it (although it took quite a bit longer to produce a resonse), but also motivated every choice, game me direct links to each component, but also gave me correct prices, and a total. It even, relatively correctly, estimated PC's performance in several games at different settings. It's all about giving a properly engineered prompt.
argument: Not only did it do it (although it took quite a bit longer to produce a resonse), but also motivated every choice

review_text:
It will defo have some negative impact on our society without proper laws and enforcing them, but so far I breathe air for free and use the AI for free on simple tasks like answering questions, or transitioning to Linux to escape the Microsoft's AI. What's not to love about it? 
As per training the AI on publicly available content - don't you get inspiration and use it in your art after you visited a gallery? Make a recognisable copy is obviously not allowed, but we all learn from what we see, hear, encounter during our lives - and pay nobody for it, do we? 
As my personal data is concerned: that is flying in the wild from countless data breaches. Nobody can do much about it, even paying to the brokers will achieve noting at the end, as the data will get out other ways.
argument: so far I breathe air for free and use the AI for free on simple tasks... What's not to love about it?

review_text:
You are using the Chinese hosted version of DeepSeek R1, so of course data goes to those servers in China.

If you downloaded the model to your computer and ran it locally, or used a version of the model hosted on US servers, then your data won’t be sent to the Chinese server.

And the model has no idea where it is hosted and where its data is being sent to. Read up a bit about how LLMs work before posting this stuff.
argument: "You are using the Chinese hosted version of DeepSeek R1, so of course data goes to those servers in China."

review_text:
How do we preserve what it means to be human when every face becomes a dataset?"
It's so romantic.
As if every face abandons its natural existence when it becomes a dataset?
That's not happening.
Another layer is added to existence.
It's just another tool.
Of course, there will be those who use it for good and those who use it for bad.

When the camera was invented, people thought it stole their souls.
It's the same thing.
argument: "'How do we preserve what it means to be human when every face becomes a dataset?' It's so romantic."
